questions.question: Station A and station C both transmit to access point B simultaneously and neither detects the collision. What is the name for this problem?
questions.explanation: The hidden terminal problem occurs when two stations that cannot hear each other both transmit to a shared receiver. Because each station's carrier sense detects only its own local environment and not the other's signal, neither detects the collision at B.

questions.question: Station B wants to send a frame to C. B senses the channel busy because it can hear A transmitting to a distant station D, even though B's own transmission would not interfere at D. What is this called?
questions.explanation: The exposed terminal problem is when a station incorrectly defers because it hears a transmission that does not actually threaten its own intended receiver. B→C cannot harm A→D, so B's deferral wastes capacity without preventing any collision.

questions.question: Given SIFS=16µs, basic rate=6Mbps, data rate=54Mbps, and a 1000-byte DATA frame, what is the NAV value placed in the RTS Duration field?
questions.explanation: NAV_RTS = SIFS + t(CTS) + SIFS + t(DATA) + SIFS + t(ACK) = 16 + 18.7 + 16 + 148.1 + 16 + 18.7 = 233.5µs. The CTS Duration (NAV_CTS) is 198.8µs because it omits the first SIFS+t(CTS) term.

questions.question: In the standard 4-frame 802.11 RTS/CTS exchange, which stations set their NAV from the CTS frame?
options: Only the station that sent the RTS
Stations that are within range of the receiver but did not hear the RTS
All stations within range of the original data sender
questions.explanation: The CTS is sent by the receiver (AP). Stations that are within range of the AP but were outside range of the original sender missed the RTS and therefore have no NAV set. Overhearing the CTS lets them set a NAV that covers the remaining DATA+SIFS+ACK exchange.

questions.question: Why does 802.11 RTS/CTS NOT cure the exposed terminal problem, while the original MACA protocol does?
options: 802.11 uses a shorter RTS frame than MACA
802.11 has stations set NAV when they hear either RTS or CTS; MACA only silences CTS hearers
802.11 RTS/CTS only works at the basic rate while MACA works at full data rate
MACA uses a longer backoff window that prevents all deferral
questions.explanation: In MACA (Karn 1990), a station that overhears an RTS does not defer — only CTS hearers defer. An exposed terminal hears an RTS but not the CTS, so under MACA it can still transmit. 802.11 silences both RTS and CTS hearers for implementation simplicity, which means exposed terminals also defer unnecessarily.

questions.question: An 802.11g network has RTS_BYTES=20 and CTS_BYTES=14, both sent at 6Mbps basic rate, with SIFS=16µs. For a 50-byte data frame sent at 54Mbps, approximately what fraction of total airtime is consumed by the RTS/CTS handshake alone?
questions.explanation: t(RTS)=(20×8)/6=26.7µs, t(CTS)=(14×8)/6=18.7µs, handshake airtime≈26.7+16+18.7+16=77.4µs. t(DATA)=(50×8)/54≈7.4µs. Handshake fraction=77.4/(77.4+7.4)≈91%. Even at a rough estimate it far exceeds 55%, which is why the RTS threshold is set high by default and RTS/CTS is not worthwhile for small frames.
